title: Vocabulary Size vs. Model Size
concept: A larger vocabulary (e.g. 250,000 tokens) reduces sequence length but significantly increases the size of the embedding layer and output classifier.

exercises.prompt: What does 'vocabulary size' refer to in an AI model?

options.text: The set of all tokens/words the model can recognize
The speed at which the model generates text
The number of layers inside the neural network

exercises.explain: A vocabulary in AI represents the set of all words, characters, or word-pieces (tokens) that the model is trained to recognize and produce.

cards.title: The Dictionary Dilemma
cards.body: Have you ever wondered how AI balances the size of its dictionary? If an AI knows too few words, it has to spell everything out in tiny pieces. But if it knows too many, its brain gets cluttered. Let's explore this delicate balance!

cards.title: Vocabulary vs. Model Size
cards.body: Vocabulary size is the total dictionary of tokens (words or word pieces) an AI knows. While a larger vocabulary lets the AI pack more meaning into fewer tokens (shorter sequences), it forces the model's 'embedding layer' and 'output classifier' to grow significantly larger to store all those words.

cards.title: The Outfit Analogy
cards.body: Imagine packing for a trip. If you pack huge, pre-assembled outfits (a large vocabulary), your suitcase has fewer items inside, so it closes easily (shorter sequence length). But your closet at home has to be massive to store all those specific, giant outfits (a huge embedding layer)!

cards.title: Comparing the Tokens
cards.body: Consider a model processing the phrase 'artificial intelligence'. With a small vocabulary, it might split this into 4 tokens: ['art', 'ific', 'ial', 'intel']. With a massive vocabulary, it might represent it as just 2 tokens: ['artificial', 'intelligence']. The sequence is shorter, but the model's dictionary size is much larger!

cards.title: Bigger Isn't Always Better
cards.body: People often think that a bigger vocabulary always makes an AI model run faster and lighter. In reality, a massive vocabulary makes the model's entry and exit gates giant, which can eat up a huge amount of computer memory!

cards.title: The Trade-off Visualized
cards.body: Let's compare how vocabulary size affects different parts of the AI model.

left.text: Longer sequences of small text pieces, but a tiny and lightweight embedding layer.

right.text: Shorter sequences of complete words, but a massive and heavy embedding layer.

exercises.prompt: What is one benefit of having a larger vocabulary in an AI model?

options.text: It decreases the sequence length of the text.
It increases the sequence length of the text.
It decreases the memory size of the embedding layer.

exercises.explain: A larger vocabulary means the AI has larger single tokens (like whole words instead of character pieces), which shortens the total sequence length of a sentence.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Increasing vocabulary size makes the model's memory footprint larger due to bigger embedding and output layers.
exercises.explain: True! Expanding the vocabulary means the model must keep track of more unique words, which directly increases the parameters in the embedding and output layers.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the explanation of vocabulary trade-offs.
exercises.sentence: A larger vocabulary reduces the length of our text sequences, but it increases the size of the entrance embedding layer.
exercises.explain: The embedding layer acts as the dictionary at the entrance of the model. A larger vocabulary requires this layer to store more word vectors, increasing its physical size.

exercises.prompt: Why don't we just give AI models an infinite vocabulary size with millions of words?

options.text: Having a giant vocabulary makes the model forget basic grammar rules.
A giant vocabulary makes the model's embedding and output layers too large for memory.
Large vocabularies force the model to process longer sequences of text.

exercises.explain: AI models do not use infinite vocabularies because storing millions of word vectors would make the model's embedding and output layers too massive to fit in computer memory.

exercises.prompt: If a team decides to increase their AI model's vocabulary size from 50,000 to 250,000 tokens, what is the major structural trade-off they must prepare for?

options.text: It decreases the number of parameters in the model.
It balloons the size of the embedding and output layers.
It makes the model process longer sequences of text.
It removes the need for an output classifier entirely.

exercises.explain: When we expand a model's vocabulary, we have to store an embedding vector for every single new word. This causes the embedding layer at the entrance and the classifier at the exit of the model to balloon in size.
